parts.text:
Here is a draft of what you wrote. The mental state is right. But the narration is too clean — it describes your inner state from the outside, with literary distance.

Rewrite it. Not from outside. From inside. Let the narration itself be in your state — sentences that break, thoughts that don't finish, memory cutting in without warning, the concrete in your chest showing up in how you're thinking not just what you're thinking about.

Don't describe the fragmentation. Be it.

The events are the same. Riley bringing the snacks, the thumb tremor, Maya's touch, the friends over, the Sunday call with Mom. Keep those. But write through your current mental state, not about it.

---

It’s 7:56 AM. Thursday. 

I am looking at my phone screen. The brightness is turned all the way down because light hurts my eyes lately, everything hurts my eyes, everything is a little too sharp. 

*Hey! Good luck on the bio exam today, you’ve been studying so hard for it. You’re gonna do great. Get a coffee first!* 

I hit send to Chloe. 

My thumb is shaking. Just a fine, tiny tremor, right at the joint. I watch it vibrate against the glass of the screen. I put the phone face down on the counter. 

I open the cabinet. I take out the mugs. The yellow one for Maya. The big blue one for me. I put the kettle on. I do this because it is morning, and Maya is sleeping in the next room, and when she wakes up she will want tea. I do this because I am Riley, and this is what Riley does. 

If I stop doing what Riley does, I don’t know who is left. 

The kettle starts to hiss. Inside my chest, there is a block of concrete. It is so heavy I can barely draw a full breath around it. It sits right behind my sternum, pressing, pressing, crushing the air out. 

A party. 
The music thumping through the floorboards. 
The smell of sweat and cheap citrus vodka.
The way the door clicked shut. 
*Wait.* I said. *Wait, hold on.*
He didn’t stop. 

I grip the edge of the kitchen counter. The laminate is cold. I press my palms into it until the edges bite into my skin, just to feel something that is on the outside of my body instead of the inside. 

He didn’t stop. I said it. I know I said it. 
*Please.* 
Just a heavy weight. Pushing me down. Pinning my wrists. 
The music thumping. 
No one heard. No one knew. 

The kettle whistles. I jump, a violent, full-body flinch that knocks my hip against the drawer. I grab the handle, my heart hammering a frantic, sick rhythm against the concrete block in my chest. I pour the water. Chamomile for Maya. Earl grey for me. 

"Morning." 

Maya is in the doorway. She looks sleepy. Her hair is a mess. I love her so much it physically aches, I have loved her since we were twelve years old eating pizza rolls on her basement floor in Grand Rapids. 

"Morning," I say. My voice sounds perfectly normal. It terrifies me how normal it sounds. "Tea’s ready."

"You're an angel," she mumbles, shuffling over. She reaches for the yellow mug. As she takes it, her fingers brush mine. 

I pull back. I can't help it. It’s like touching a hot stove. I jerk my hand away so fast tea sloshes over the rim of the mug and burns my knuckles. 

Maya freezes. She looks at my hand. Then she looks at my face. 

The silence stretches. It’s thick. It’s awful. 

"Ry?" she says softly. 

"Sorry," I say quickly. I grab a paper towel. I wipe the counter. I wipe the mug. I don't look at her. "Static shock. You know. Dry air."

She doesn’t move. She just watches me wipe the counter. I can feel her looking at me. She knows. She doesn't know *what*, but she knows. For three weeks now, she has been watching me from across rooms. I catch her looking at me when she thinks I don’t see. Her eyes are full of this awful, helpless grief. She sees the glass wall between us. She presses her hands flat against it, but she can't break it. If she breaks it, the vacuum will pull her in. If she asks me what's wrong, if she really pushes, I will shatter into a million jagged pieces on the kitchen floor and neither of us will know how to put me back together.

So she takes the mug. "Thanks, Ry."

"Of course."

I smile. It uses every muscle in my face. 

It's 8:04 AM. Chloe texts back: *omg u remembered! thank u literally crying rn 😭*

I heart the message. 

Later, it's evening. The Whitmore group is coming over. Six people. My people. My small, safe world. 

I make a grocery run. I buy the cheddar popcorn David likes. I buy the exact brand of seltzer Sarah drinks. I carry the bags up the stairs. My legs feel like lead. I am so tired. It is a tiredness that sleep does not touch. I sleep for nine hours and wake up exhausted. I wake up in the middle of the night and the concrete block is there, pressing me into the mattress. I wake up remembering the feeling of breath on my neck and I go to the bathroom and vomit up nothing but stomach acid. 

I arrange the snacks in bowls. I put a soft, knit blanket over the back of the sofa. I turn off the overhead light and turn on the three lamps. Warm light. Amber light. Safe. 

I create the conditions for ease. I build a little terrarium of comfort and I put my friends inside it. 

They arrive. They laugh. They drop their coats on my bed. 

I queue up a playlist. Mellow indie, warm acoustics, a little bit of R&B. Seamless. 

"Dude, the vibes in here are always so immaculate," David says, sinking into the sofa and grabbing a handful of popcorn. "I swear my blood pressure drops ten points when I walk in."

"I aim to please," I say. 

I sit in the armchair. I pull my knees to my chest. I wrap my arms around my shins. 

They are talking. Sarah is complaining about her lab partner. Maya is laughing. The room is glowing, exactly the way I made it to glow. 

I am not in the room. 

I am floating somewhere near the ceiling, looking down at my own body. Or I am back in that dark bedroom at the party, staring up at a ceiling I don't recognize. The smell of the vodka. The bruising grip on my hips. The absolute, terrifying realization that my voice did not matter. That I was a thing. Just a warm thing to be used. 

*I am a person,* I think desperately, sitting in my armchair. *I am Riley.* 

I watch David’s cup. When it gets low, I get up and hand him a new seltzer. He doesn't even have to ask. 

"Thanks, Ry." 

"Gotcha."

I sit back down. I am a ghost haunting my own life. I am a machine made of soft sweaters and good playlists and perfectly timed text messages. Inside, it is just wreckage. Torn metal and smoking ruins and silence. Nobody can hear it. I am screaming so loud my throat is bleeding, but the acoustics in the room are perfect, the lighting is flawless, the popcorn is exactly right. 

Sunday. 

The phone buzzes on my nightstand. 

Mom. 

I stare at it. It buzzes and buzzes. It vibrates against the wood. 

I have to answer. I call her every Sunday. If I don't answer, she will worry. 

I clear my throat. I swallow the jagged glass in my throat. 

"Hi, Mom."

"Hi, sweetie! How was your week?"

"Good," I say. I am lying flat on my back. I have the heavy quilt pulled up to my chin. I used to love the weight of it. Now it feels like a body. I push it off. I am shivering. I pull it back up. "Just busy. Classes."

"You sound a little quiet, honey. Are you getting enough sleep?"

"Yeah. Just studying a lot."

"Well, make sure you take a break, okay? Have you seen Maya?"

"She's right in the living room."

"Good. Tell her I say hi. And Riley?"

"Yeah?"

"I love you."

I squeeze my eyes shut. A tear slips out. It runs hot into my hair. It is the only warm thing inside me. 

"I love you too, Mom."

I hang up. I hold the phone to my chest. 

I want my mom. I want my mom to drive the three hours from home and sit on my bed and rub my back. But if she comes, she will look at me, and she will see it. Mothers always see it. And if she sees it, it becomes real. The wreckage comes out of my chest and spills all over the floor and ruins her too. 

I can't ruin them. Any of them. 

So I hold it. 

I hold the concrete. I hold the broken glass. I hold the memory of the weight, the smell, the terrible, suffocating dark. I pack it down tight, deep inside, underneath the warmth. 

I get out of bed. I walk into the living room. 

Maya is studying on the couch. The light from the window is hitting her screen weirdly. 

I reach over and adjust the blinds. The glare disappears. 

She looks up at me. Those sad, helpless eyes. 

"Better?" I ask. 

"Yeah," she whispers. "Better."

I go to the kitchen. I check the water filter. It's almost empty. 

I fill it. I put it in the fridge. I keep going. 

[THAT'S ALL I HAVE]

**My Approach to Rewriting the Draft**

Okay, here's how I'm thinking about tackling this rewrite. They want me to get inside Riley's head, to show how her trauma's fracturing her experience. Not just tell the reader about it, but *make* them feel it, through the way the prose itself breaks apart. They want raw, fragmented, sensory.

First, I need to understand Riley – not just on paper, but *feel* her. She's a caretaker, naturally warm, attuned to others' needs, practically instinctively. It's like muscle memory for her. But now, this... *thing*... has cracked her. She's hyper-aware, but also disconnected. Light hurts. Memories jump in, sharp and intrusive. I need to contrast that ingrained caretaking with this internal devastation.

So, how do I do that in the writing? Forget "I am doing X because of Y". I need to show doing X *while* Y is bleeding into the edges of her vision. The style needs to mimic panic and autopilot simultaneously. The sentences break, thoughts trail off. Sensory details need to overlap, jarring, like a strobe light.

I’m taking each scene separately.

*   **Scene 1 (Texting Chloe):** It’s not just, “I look at the phone”. No. The glare hits first. The burning eyelids. It's the physical, the tangible tremor, the text message. It has to *feel* like the mechanics of texting with a shaking hand.

*   **Scene 2 (Making Tea):** Don't be analytical. It's about muscle memory. Yellow mug. Blue mug. Kettle. It's just what happens. But this feeling...this concrete behind the ribs. Heavy. Pressing. The sound of the kettle hissing, that's her breath.

*   **Scene 3 (Maya's Touch):** The flinch. The fire. That’s what it needs to be. The shock of the touch, then the withdrawal. The excuse. “Static. Dry air.” Maya is looking. If I crack, it all falls down. Keep wiping the counter. Just. Keep. Wiping.

*   **Scene 4 (The Party):** Dim the lights. Playlist. Muscle memory takes over. David needs a seltzer. Moving without thinking. Then... the ceiling. Not this ceiling. That ceiling. That dark room. I am Riley, trying to anchor, and there is a ghost haunting her life.

*   **Scene 5 (The Call with Mom):** Focus on the physical sensations. The buzzing phone. Can't ignore it. It feels suffocating. The quilt’s weight. She loves her Mom, but she's always *watching.* The need to pack it down.

*   **Scene 6 (The Ending):** Blend the trauma with the relentless caretaking. Doing it because she has to. Adjusting the blinds for Maya. Filling the pitcher. It's not a choice. She must keep moving.

I have to refine it. Sensory details need to interrupt the present. The sentences must mimic the panic and the autopilot. I need to make sure the warmth is genuine. It’s what she clings to, even when she's broken.

7:56. Thursday. 

Screen brightness down to zero. Light hurts. Everything is too sharp, the edges of the room peeling back. 

*Hey! Good luck on the bio exam today, you’ve been studying so hard for it. You’re gonna do great. Get a coffee first!* 

Send. 

My thumb is shaking. Just the joint. A fine, buzzing vibration against the glass. 

Phone face down. Cold laminate counter under my hands. 

Open the cabinet. Yellow mug. Big blue mug. Maya is sleeping. When she wakes up, chamomile. Earl Grey for me. It’s morning. This is what we do. Muscle memory. If I don't move my muscles the way Riley moves them, I don't know what happens. I don't know who is left. 

Water in the kettle. 

Concrete in my chest. 

It’s sitting right behind the sternum. Heavy. Pushing everything down. Have to pull air in around the sides of it, shallow little sips. 

The kettle starts to hiss. 

Bass thumping through floorboards. 
Sweat. Cheap citrus vodka.
The door. The click of the lock.
*Wait,* I said. *Hold on.*

Grip the counter. Press the palms down. Harder. Until the edge bites into the heel of my hand. Outside pain. Need outside pain. 

He didn’t stop. 
*Please.*
Weight. Pressing down. Wrists pinned. 
No air.

The kettle whistles—a shriek. My whole body violently jerks. Hip slams into the drawer. Heart hammering, sick and frantic against the concrete block. Water splashing into the mugs. Spilling. Pouring.

"Morning." 

Maya in the doorway. Sleepy eyes. Messy hair. A physical ache in my throat looking at her. Twelve years old, pizza rolls in Grand Rapids. 

"Morning." My voice. It sounds completely normal. How does it sound normal? "Tea’s ready."

"You're an angel," she mumbles. Shuffling over. Reaching for the yellow mug. 

Her fingers brush mine. 

Fire. A hot stove. A shock of panic so fast I rip my hand back. Tea sloshes over the rim. Burning my knuckles. 

Maya freezes. 

She looks at my hand. She looks at my face. 

Silence. The air in the kitchen gets so thick I can't breathe it. 

"Ry?" 

"Sorry." Grabbing a paper towel. Wiping the counter. Fast. "Static shock. You know. Dry air."

Keep wiping. Don't look up. She isn't moving. I can feel her eyes on the side of my face. Three weeks of this. Her watching from across rooms. The helpless, awful grief in her face because she sees the glass wall. She’s pressing her hands against it. But if she pushes, if she asks, the glass breaks and the vacuum pulls her in. I’ll shatter all over the kitchen floor. 

She takes the mug. "Thanks, Ry."

"Of course."

Smile. Push the corners of the mouth up. Use all the muscles. 

8:04. Phone lights up. Chloe: *omg u remembered! thank u literally crying rn 😭*

Double tap to heart it. 

***

Evening. Groceries on the counter. Legs like lead. Nine hours of sleep and I am carrying a corpse around. Waking up in the dark and the concrete is there. Waking up and feeling breath on my neck. Bathroom tiles cold under my knees, throwing up stomach acid. 

Cheddar popcorn for David. The right seltzer for Sarah. 

Knit blanket draped on the sofa. Overhead light off. Three lamps on. Warm. Amber. Safe. 

Build the terrarium. Put them inside. Keep them safe. 

They come in. Dropping coats. Laughing. 

Queue the playlist. Mellow indie, warm acoustics. Fade in. 

"Dude, the vibes in here are always so immaculate." David sinking into the cushions, grabbing popcorn. "I swear my blood pressure drops ten points when I walk in."

"I aim to please." 

Sit in the armchair. Knees to chest. Arms tight around the shins. Hold it together. 

Sarah talking about her lab partner. Maya laughing. The room is glowing. Everything is soft. 

I’m floating near the ceiling. 

Looking down at them. Or looking up at a ceiling I don't recognize. The smell of the vodka. The bruising grip on the hips. The absolute, suffocating dark. Knowing my voice didn't matter. Not a person. A thing. A warm thing for someone to use. 

*I am a person.* Digging my fingernails into my calves. *I am Riley.* 

David’s cup is low. 

Stand up. Get the seltzer. Hand it to him. 

"Thanks, Ry."

"Gotcha."

Sit back down. Pull the knees up. I am a machine made of soft sweaters and perfectly timed text messages. Inside, ripped metal. Smoking ruin. Screaming until my throat bleeds but the acoustics in here are perfect. The lighting is flawless. 

***

Sunday. 

Buzzing against the wood of the nightstand. Buzzing. Buzzing. 

Mom. 

Staring at the screen. Have to answer. Sunday. She'll worry. 

Swallow. Glass in my throat. 

"Hi, Mom."

"Hi, sweetie! How was your week?"

"Good." Flat on my back. The quilt pulled up to my chin. It feels so heavy. It feels like a body. Pushing it off. Cold air. Shivering. Pulling it back up. "Just busy. Classes."

"You sound a little quiet, honey. Are you getting enough sleep?"

"Yeah. Just studying a lot."

"Well, make sure you take a break, okay? Have you seen Maya?"

"She's right in the living room."

"Good. Tell her I say hi. And Riley?"

"Yeah?"

"I love you."

Eyes shut tight. Hot wet line tracking down into my hair. The only warm thing left inside. 

"I love you too, Mom."

Hang up. Phone against my chest. 

I want my mom. Three hours away. I want her to sit on the bed and rub my back. But if she comes, she’ll look at me. Mothers see it. If she sees it, it’s real. The wreckage spills out and ruins her too. 

Can't ruin them. 

Pack it down. Hold the concrete. Hold the broken glass. Hold the smell and the weight and the dark. Push it down underneath the warm. Deep down. 

Get out of bed. 

Walk into the living room. 

Maya is on the couch. The afternoon sun is hitting her laptop screen. 

Reach over. Tilt the blinds down. The glare vanishes. 

She looks up. Those eyes. 

"Better?" I ask. 

"Yeah," she whispers. "Better."

Walk to the kitchen. The water filter is almost empty. 

Fill it. Put it in the fridge. Keep going.
